records.ety: [F. justifier, L. justificare; justus just + -ficare (in comp.) to make. See Just, a., and -fy.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Justified (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Justifying (?).]

defs: To prove or show to be just; to vindicate; to maintain or defend as conformable to law, right, justice, propriety, or duty.
quotes: That to the height of this great argument I may assert eternal providence, And justify the ways of God to men.
Unless the oppression is so extreme as to justify revolution, it would not justify the evil of breaking up a government.

defs: To pronounce free from guilt or blame; to declare or prove to have done that which is just, right, proper, etc.; to absolve; to exonerate; to clear.
quotes: I can not justify whom the law condemns.

defs: To treat as if righteous and just; to pardon; to exculpate; to absolve.
quotes: By him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses.

defs: To prove; to ratify; to confirm.

To make even or true, as lines of type, by proper spacing; to align (text) at the left (left justify) or right (right justify) margins of a column or page, or at both margins; to adjust, as type. See Justification, 4.
To show (a person) to have had a sufficient legal reason for an act that has been made the subject of a charge or accusation.
To qualify (one's self) as a surety by taking oath to the ownership of sufficient property.
quotes: The production of bail in court, who there justify themselves against the exception of the plaintiff.

defs: To form an even surface or true line with something else; to fit exactly.
To take oath to the ownership of property sufficient to qualify one's self as bail or surety.
